title: What Is Keycloak?
description:
## Identity and Access Management, Done for You

**Keycloak** is an open-source **Identity and Access Management (IAM)** server. Instead of every application building its own login screen, password storage, and session handling, your apps **delegate authentication to Keycloak** and trust the tokens it issues. This gives you centralized **Single Sign-On (SSO)**: a user signs in once and can reach many applications without logging in again.

Keycloak speaks the industry-standard protocols so almost any app or library can integrate with it:

| Protocol | What it's for |
|---|---|
| **OpenID Connect (OIDC)** | Modern authentication layer built on OAuth 2.0 — the default for web/mobile/SPA apps |
| **OAuth 2.0** | Delegated authorization / issuing access tokens for APIs |
| **SAML 2.0** | Legacy/enterprise SSO, often for older or B2B applications |

### Core concepts you will meet constantly

| Term | What it is |
|---|---|
| **Realm** | An isolated tenant — its own users, clients, roles, and signing keys. Everything lives inside a realm. |
| **Client** | An application or service that asks Keycloak to authenticate users (or that validates tokens). |
| **User** | An account that can log in: credentials, profile attributes, role assignments. |
| **Role** | A named permission (`admin`, `viewer`) that you map to users or groups. |
| **Group** | A collection of users that can carry role mappings and attributes, for bulk management. |

### Two built-in UIs

- **Admin Console** — where operators configure realms, clients, users, roles, flows, and policies.
- **Account Console** — where end users manage their own profile, password, MFA devices, and active sessions.

> **Mental model:** Keycloak is the *trusted front door*. Apps don't check passwords themselves — they redirect users to Keycloak, and then trust the signed **tokens** Keycloak hands back.

This Keycloak track sits alongside the platform's broader **DevOps** and **AWS** subjects, so you can connect identity concepts to the systems you build and deploy. When the vocabulary feels familiar, mark this lesson complete and continue.
